1: Transitional formula — YHWH continues speaking to Moses. The divine appointment of artisans for the tabernacle follows immediately after the material specifications of Exodus 30. The sequence is significant: YHWH does not merely specify what he wants built; he also identifies and equips the builders. The commissioning of Bezalel and Oholiab is as much part of the divine revelation as the architectural plans.
2: Bezalel son of Uri son of Hur — from the tribe of Judah. YHWH calls him by name, as he called Moses by name (Exodus 3:4). Bezalel's lineage: Judah (the royal tribe), grandson of Hur (who held up Moses's hands at Rephidim, Exodus 17:10-12; likely the Hur of 1 Chronicles 2:19-20). The tribal identity (Judah) and the family connection (grandson of YHWH's servant Hur) legitimate Bezalel's appointment. The divine calling of a craftsman by name establishes artistic vocation as genuine divine calling, not mere technical employment.
3: “I have filled him with the Spirit of God (ruach Elohim), with skill (chokhmah), understanding (tevunah), and knowledge (da'at) in every kind of craft.” The first occurrence of “filled with the Spirit of God” in the Torah — and it is for an artisan, not a prophet or warrior. The Spirit's first described filling is for the creation of sacred art. Paul's lists of spiritual gifts (1 Cor 12; Rom 12) include practical service functions alongside prophetic and teaching gifts. The three cognitive gifts — wisdom, understanding, knowledge — are the same triad as Proverbs 3:19-20 (YHWH by wisdom founded the earth, by understanding established the heavens, by knowledge split the deep). Bezalel's Spirit-filled crafting mirrors YHWH's Spirit-filled creating.
4: Gold, silver, and bronze — the three metals of the tabernacle in descending value and decreasing sanctity from the Most Holy Place outward. Artistic design (machshavot) — planned, intentional patterns, not random decoration. The same word is used for human thought/planning (Genesis 50:20) and for YHWH's plans (Jeremiah 29:11).
5: Cutting and setting stones, carving wood — the full range of fine craftwork required for the ephod's stones (Exodus 28:9-11), the breastpiece gems (Exodus 28:17-20), and the wooden frames and furnishings. The Spirit's equipping for tabernacle work is exhaustive: every craft Bezalel will need has been divinely supplied.
6: Oholiab son of Ahisamach from Dan — the second named artisan. The pairing of Judah (Bezalel) and Dan (Oholiab) covers the first and last tribes in the camp arrangement of Num 2. This may suggest that the tabernacle project gathers the whole nation under its service. “I have given skill to all the craftsmen” — the Spirit's distribution is not limited to the named two; the entire skilled workforce is Spirit-equipped.
7: The tent of meeting, the ark with the atonement cover — the most sacred furnishings listed first. The ark is the structural center of the tabernacle system; its atonement cover (kapporet) is where YHWH meets with Moses (Exodus 25:22). The comprehensive list of what Bezalel and Oholiab will make (Exodus 31:7-11) mirrors the comprehensive instructions of Exodus 25-30.
8: The table and its articles, the pure gold lampstand (menorah) and all its accessories, the altar of incense — the holy place furnishings. The three furnishings of the holy place form a complementary unit: the table provides bread (presence-offering), the lampstand provides light, the incense altar provides fragrance — food, light, and prayer surrounding the veil of the Most Holy Place.
9: The altar of burnt offering with all its utensils, the basin with its stand — the outer court furnishings. The full extent of Bezalel's and Oholiab's work covers both the interior (holy place and Most Holy Place) and the exterior (outer court) of the sanctuary system.
10: The woven garments — the priestly vestments of Exodus 28-29. The artisans who make the sanctuary also make the priestly clothing — the human wearer and the sacred space are both covered by the Spirit-equipped craftsmen's work.
11: The anointing oil and the fragrant incense — the two proprietary sacred substances of Exodus 30. The concluding phrase: “They are to make them just as I commanded you.” When Exodus 35-40 record the tabernacle's construction, the repeated formula “just as YHWH commanded Moses” (used 18 times in Exodus 39-40) demonstrates that Bezalel and the craftsmen fulfilled this commission exactly.
12: The Sabbath commandment inserted at the end of the tabernacle instructions — YHWH emphasizes Sabbath precisely when commissioning the greatest building project in Israel's history. The juxtaposition is intentional: no amount of sacred work overrides the sacred rest. The rabbinic tradition later derived the 39 categories of prohibited work from the 39 types of labor required for tabernacle construction.
13: “You must observe my Sabbaths. This will be a sign (ot) between me and you for the generations to come, so you may know that I am YHWH who makes you holy.” The Sabbath as covenant sign (ot) — the same word used for circumcision (Genesis 17:11) and the rainbow (Genesis 9:12-13). The Sabbath is Israel's temporal covenant-identity marker as circumcision is the bodily one. “YHWH who makes you holy (mequaddishchem)” — the Sabbath's sanctification of time mirrors YHWH's sanctification of Israel.
14: “Observe the Sabbath, because it is holy to you. Anyone who desecrates it is to be put to death.” The death penalty for Sabbath violation reflects the Sabbath's status as a covenant sign — violating the sign violates the covenant. Numbers 15:32-36 records the execution of a Sabbath wood-gatherer as the concrete application of this law.
15: “A Sabbath of complete rest (shabbat shabbaton), holy to YHWH” — the doubled term for maximum emphasis. The rabbinic tradition derived the 39 categories of prohibited work from the 39 types of labor required for tabernacle construction, meaning exactly the work now being commissioned is the definition of what must not be done on the Sabbath.
16: “The Israelites are to observe the Sabbath, keeping it as a lasting covenant (berit olam).” The Sabbath is explicitly designated as a covenant — not merely a commandment or a custom. The same designation (berit olam) applies to the Abrahamic covenant (Genesis 17:7), the Noahic covenant (Genesis 9:16), and the Davidic covenant (2 Samuel 23:5). The Sabbath joins the foundational covenants as a permanent covenantal structure.
17: “It will be a sign between me and the Israelites forever, because in six days YHWH made the heavens and the earth, and on the seventh day he rested and was refreshed (vayinnafash).” Nefesh (breath/soul/refreshment) — the word for the human person (“soul”) is also the word for the refreshment YHWH takes on the seventh day. YHWH rested and was refreshed as a being with nefesh. Israel's Sabbath participates in YHWH's own creational rhythm of work and rest.
18: The two tablets of the covenant law, inscribed by the finger of God (etzbah Elohim) — the direct divine authorship of the Decalogue. “Finger of God” is also used for the plagues (Exodus 8:19) and for Jesus's casting out of demons (Luke 11:20). The tablets given here are shattered in 32:19 because the covenant they represent is being violated at the very moment of their giving — the deepest possible illustration of human sinfulness.
